title: Air-fryer pork belly
subtitle: Crisp crackling pork belly with five spice
excerpt: Pork belly seasoned with Chinese five spice, salt, and garlic powder, cooked in the air fryer until the flesh is tender and the skin is shatteringly crisp. Better crackling than most ovens manage in twice the time. Serves four.
sourceNotes: Pork belly is the standard cut for air-fryer crackling because the thick fat layer renders during cooking, self-basting the meat while the circulating air desiccates and puffs the skin. The Chinese five spice seasoning draws on Cantonese crispy pork belly (siu yuk) technique, where the spice is rubbed into the flesh side while the skin is kept dry and scored for maximum crackling.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Score the skin and leave uncovered in the fridge overnight for drier skin and better crackling.

content.text: The two rules for good air-fryer crackling are: dry skin and salt only on top. The five spice and garlic powder go on the flesh side, pressed into the scored cuts. The skin gets a thin layer of fine salt and nothing else. Any moisture on the skin, or any oil or spice, will prevent the crackling from puffing. Leave the belly uncovered in the fridge overnight if you can.

items.prepNote: skin scored at 1 cm intervals, dried thoroughly

content.text: Pat the pork belly completely dry, especially the skin. Score the skin at 1 cm intervals with a sharp knife, cutting just through the skin into the fat but not into the flesh. Mix {{chinese-five-spice}} of five spice, {{garlic-powder}} of garlic powder, {{black-pepper}} of black pepper, and 1 teaspoon of the salt. Rub this mixture all over the flesh side and into the scored flesh. Rub the remaining 0.5 teaspoon of salt over the skin. Leave uncovered in the fridge for at least 1 hour, or overnight. Preheat the air fryer to 200°C. Place the belly skin-side up in the basket. Cook for 35–40 minutes until the skin is puffed and shatteringly crisp. Rest for 5 minutes before slicing.

items.symptom: Skin blistered in places but flat and chewy in others
items.cause: Uneven scoring, or the skin was not flat in the basket.
items.fix: Score every centimetre consistently. If the belly curves up, weigh it flat with a small ovenproof dish in the basket.

items.cause: Too much moisture in the skin, or the air fryer temperature too low.
items.fix: Pat dry again before cooking. Increase to 210°C for the final 10 minutes to drive off residual moisture and puff the skin.

items.cause: Belly too thin (under 3 cm), or cooked too long.
items.fix: Thinner pieces cook faster. Check at 25 minutes and pull when the flesh is cooked through — crackling can continue at 210°C for just the last 5 minutes.

content.text: Leaving the scored pork belly uncovered in the fridge draws moisture out of the skin through osmosis and evaporation. The skin visibly changes texture overnight: it dries to a papery feel, compared to the damp, slightly sticky feel of fresh-from-the-butcher skin. This drier starting point means the air fryer has less work to do, and the crackling is more even across the whole surface.
